words: This second interview with Eugene Settle takes place at his home in Moscow, Idaho
on July 7, 1975. (Sam Schrager)
EUGENE SETTLE: Well, I was pretty young.
SAM SCHRAGER: Well, how old were you?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I was abo.ut twenty-two, twenty-three when I went in. But I

speaker: ES
words: was real small, I only weighed about a hundred and twenty-eight

speaker: ES
words: pounds, stripped. So, you see, there wasn't much of me, at that age.

speaker: SS
words: How did you happen to enlist?

speaker: ES
words: I was drafted. Yeah, I went to Spokane to enlist. I had a friend who

speaker: ES
words: was in the navy, he was a fireman. He enlisted as a fireman and after

speaker: ES
words: he went in, he kept writing to me to come on and enlist in the navy as

speaker: ES
words: a fireman and we might be lucky enough to get on the same boat. So .

speaker: ES
words: in October f17, I went up to enlist, and they took me alright, but they

speaker: ES
words: couldn't put me in what I wanted to get in, because I wasn't heavy

speaker: ES
words: enough. I think you had to weigh a hundred, I think a hundred and thir

speaker: ES
words: ty-five, stripped, to enlist as a fireman, and I only weighed a hundred

speaker: ES
words: and twenty-eight. But they told me I could go in in what branches there

speaker: ES
words: was, and (if) I gained weight, why then I could transfer

speaker: ES
words: But I didn't do it. I didn't do it, I just thought well, I come on home

speaker: ES
words: and waithfor the draft about six months later. And I was in Camp Lewis

speaker: ES
words: about, long enough to get my shots. I was there just about a month,

speaker: ES
words: six weeks maybe. And I was transferred to Camp Funston.

speaker: SS
words: Camp what

speaker: ES
words: Camp Funston, Kansas to help make up another outfit that was ready to

speaker: ES
words: go overseas. And so we went over there from Camp Funston to Camp Merrit

speaker: ES
words: and was there about three days and we went overseas. Then we went

speaker: ES
words: overseas and went up to Verdun. The Battle of Verdun was over with but

speaker: ES
words: it was still smoldering around there. And that's where they split us

speaker: ES
words: all up. Some of the boys moved over to a replacement outfit, some of

speaker: ES
words: the guys they put in the ammunition train and some went in— transfer

speaker: ES
words: red to different branches of the service. And you take— there was a

speaker: ES
words: hundred— about a hundred and sixty of us guys that went to Camp Lewis—

speaker: ES
words: we hadn't had any training you might say at all— that is, we drilled

speaker: ES
words: alright, but a lot of the guys hadn't been on a rifle range. Some of

speaker: ES
words: those kids out of the city, you know, they never saw a gun, you might

speaker: ES
words: say,until they got out there. But they divided us up. They'd take some

speaker: ES
words: of the guys and some of them they sent to ammunition train and one

speaker: ES
words: thing and another. Of course, I was one of the guys— they sent me

speaker: ES
words: and about fifteen or twenty other fellows, we went out to a little town

speaker: ES
words: to do some advanced training. And so we went out there and took our

speaker: ES
words: gas mask training and some rifle and pistol training. And then along

speaker: ES
words: about in October, the latter part of October, then they called us up.

speaker: ES
words: They called us up to join our outfits. We was just getting just about

speaker: ES
words: towards the end of the war,

speaker: ES
words: bogged down up there,ater, rain and mud, tney needed reinforcements

speaker: ES
words: and they was calling up everybody behind the lines then. So that s

speaker: ES
words: when I went up. And I didn't have to go then, my lieutenant told me,

speaker: ES
words: "You don't have to go." But, I wanted to go, so I went. And we got

speaker: ES
words: up there in the Argonne Woods, and we was up there— we was supposed

speaker: ES
words: to have got up there on the evening of the 10th— no, I guess it was

speaker: ES
words: the 9th, because the next day we was in there, they dispersed us ammun

speaker: ES
words: ition, tents. They issued us ammunition and inspected clogtags and ev

speaker: ES
words: erything, and we was supposed to go in on the night of the 11th. And

speaker: ES
words: the morning of the 11th the Armistice was signed, so you see how close

speaker: ES
words: I missed it. Instead of going in, that night we turned around, they

speaker: ES
words: turned us around and started us— well, it was on the night of the 10ththe

speaker: ES
words: evening of the 11th, they turned us around. We was to go in tnat

speaker: ES
words: night, and instead of going ahead, they just give us about face and

speaker: ES
words: started us on back again, and the Armistice was signed the next morning.

speaker: SS
words: How did you feel about missing the combat? Were you relieved?

speaker: ES
words: Well,I kind of wanted to get in. I got under artillery fire, but I nev

speaker: ES
words: er got under I'd kind of liked to have got under fire. I was glad

speaker: ES
words: I got as close as I did. Htfhen I got'in the army, I wanted to get in

speaker: ES
words: combat, and I wanted to get wounded, so I could have a Purple Heart.

speaker: ES
words: That was my desire, I never did think I was gonna get killed. I didn't

speaker: ES
words: feel that way about it, I never did think they was gonna kill me! But

speaker: ES
words: I wanted to get wounded and that's I could get a Purple Heart!!

speaker: SS
words: Were the chances pretty good of that if you got in combat?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah, I think so. Pretty good. 'Course, when we was up there, when

speaker: ES
words: we was up there just before the Armistice was signed—it was a running

speaker: ES
words: battle, you know. The Germans was all in retreat and we was after 'em.

speaker: ES
words: And in the Argonne woods there, they would put a machine gun nest

speaker: ES
words: over here and one over here, just behind 'em to hold us back, see, while

speaker: ES
words: the main army was getting away. That's the way they did it. They'd

speaker: ES
words: have probably five or six men there fighting a whole company with machine

speaker: ES
words: Armistice was signed

speaker: ES
words: guns. And, of course, they tell me that we was losing about six men

speaker: ES
words: to one at that rate, because they was covering so many of us with so

speaker: ES
words: few of them, with machine guns. So, I don't know, I might have got a

speaker: ES
words: not ever have Of course

speaker: ES
words: Purple Heart and ver been able to wear it!! lots of them got

speaker: ES
words: rifle wounds too, as far as that's concerned, but the biggest majority

speaker: ES
words: of the wounded was from shrapnel.

speaker: SS
words: What was it like in the camps there? The army camps in Europe?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I'll tell you. We got into Brest,France and it was raining and

speaker: ES
words: they put us out there just building the camp there at Brest when I got

speaker: ES
words: in there, September. And it was just a mud hole. We got in there one

speaker: ES
words: evening and we slept in the mud that night and the next night they had

speaker: ES
words: the barracks finished enough so we could get in out of the mud. And

speaker: ES
words: we just got into it and got our wet blankets spread out on the floor

speaker: ES
words: and then the officer stepped in there and said, "Be ready to roll in

speaker: ES
words: half an hour." So I didn't get to spend no night in no camp. But after

speaker: ES
words: the Armistice I came back as far as Brest— not Brest, but Longuyon,

speaker: ES
words: France. We followed the Army of Occupation that was going up on the

speaker: ES
words: Rhine, we followed them back to Longuyon, France, that's just out of

speaker: ES
words: Belgium. We camped there,and that's where I spent the winter of 1919.

speaker: ES
words: I thought I was gonna get to come home, but I didn't. So, there was

speaker: ES
words: one company of— 139th Infantry there— there was a veterinary company

speaker: ES
words: there, they had a lot of wounded and gassed horses there they was doc

speaker: ES
words: toring. And there was a company— there was three companies there—

speaker: ES
words: two companies of American soldiers, one infantry outfit, one veterinary

speaker: ES
words: outfit and one French company there. But the French company didn't

speaker: ES
words: stay there all winter, they just stayed there 'til after Christmas and

speaker: ES
words: I imagine they went home. That's about the only camp I was in. I spent

speaker: ES
words: the winter there. It was pretty good. We had pitched barracks and it

speaker: ES
words: wasn't too bad.

speaker: SS
words: What about the time when you were on advanced training?

speaker: ES
words: When I was on advanced training over there, we stayed in a little French

speaker: ES
words: village and where we was, we slept in sheds and barns there in the coun

speaker: ES
words: try. My quarters was up overhead in an attic over a French home, where

speaker: ES
words: I stayed. Four- of us stayed there. We did guard duty every night there.

speaker: ES
words: The road went right through this little village and we had to guard the

speaker: ES
words: entrance to the village all the time. He just had to walk from one end

speaker: ES
words: or it to the other to see what was going on, you know, feach guard post

speaker: ES
words: l every hour. And so that's all we did. And then they praca

speaker: ES
words: ticed putting on gas masks.We had a chamber full of gas and then we'd

speaker: ES
words: have to put on our gas masks and go through it and train that way. It

speaker: ES
words: was a pretty soft life.

speaker: SS
words: For the middle of the war.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, for the middle of the war.

speaker: SS
words: Was the company integrated?

speaker: ES
words: No. No, it was all black.

speaker: SS
words: Were the officers black, too?

speaker: ES
words: No, most all of the officers were white. All white. About the only

speaker: ES
words: black officers I saw while 1 was over in France, they were on the med

speaker: ES
words: ical staff or dental, something like that. Doctors. But the officers-

speaker: ES
words: I don't believe— I know there wasn't any in our outfit. There was—

speaker: ES
words: I think in what they called the— what they called the Quartermaster

speaker: ES
words: Corps. I believe in the Quartermaster Corps, I think they had black

speaker: ES
words: officers. They didn't do nothing, they unloaded ships and stuff like

speaker: ES
words: that, you know. I think there was some.A.officers in the Quartermaster

speaker: ES
words: Corps.

speaker: SS
words: Where did most of the guys come from? Were they Westerners?

speaker: ES
words: Well, no. The outfit I was in, most of 'em were from Texas. I went

speaker: ES
words: from here to Camp Lewis, and of course, the boys I was with in Camp

speaker: ES
words: lewis was from Montana, Idaho and California. Most of 'em was from

speaker: ES
words: California. And then when we left Fort Lewis and went to Camp Funston,

speaker: ES
words: Kansas; that s when the Texas, from Camp Travis, Texas was up there.

speaker: ES
words: They were ready to go over,Athey wasn t up to fighting strength, you

speaker: ES
words: know, so they took a hundred and fifty of us up here at Camp Lewis%to

speaker: ES
words: fill 'em in. And then after we got filled in— we just— We started

speaker: ES
words: out on rifle range there one morning and we hadn't got very far —and we

speaker: ES
words: back to get ready to sail got  we went from there to Camp Merritt, New Jersey.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know when you were going in there that that's the way that it

speaker: SS
words: was? That it wasn't going to be integrated?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, yeah, I knew that. I knew that before I went in. When I was

speaker: ES
words: drafted here, it came out in the paper that I would be sent to— I

speaker: ES
words: be able to they had me

speaker: ES
words: wouldn't go with the Moscow boys because sent to some camp

speaker: ES
words: in the South. Some of 'em tried to do something about it, but it didn't

speaker: ES
words: work out. So it worked out pretty well for me, anyway. I got in a

speaker: ES
words: pretty good outfit, the 139th Infantry, what little time I was up front.

speaker: ES
words: And then when that was over, a bunch of guys come in from the last draft

speaker: ES
words: they brought them over there,

speaker: ES
words: I guess, and they were dismantling airplanes and stuff like that and

speaker: ES
words: tearing down buildings. And so I was transferred over there in the

speaker: ES
words: spring. The main division that I was with, they came home the first of

speaker: ES
words: the year, you see, and these guys like myself that got in there late—

speaker: ES
words: and I was a sergeant—. That s one thing there—they needed noncom

speaker: ES
words: officer with this new bunch that come in, so I got pulled into that.

speaker: ES
words: then we went out to Romainesv-France, had a big German prison camp there,

speaker: ES
words: guard duty- And when I was out there— I was not there but a little

speaker: ES
words: while and I was a pretty good athlete in school, and then they started

speaker: ES
words: to get up a track team and so I got on the track team. From March 'til

speaker: ES
words: the First of July I didn't do nothing but just travel all over France,

speaker: ES
words: wherever there was American troops, this track team went. On Decoration

speaker: ES
words: Day in 1919 I ran in a relay race from Chateau to Paris and the race

speaker: ES
words: ended at the Arch of Triumphe in Paris and I ran the last three miles

speaker: ES
words: That was on Decoration Day, 1919. And so, they called us back about

speaker: ES
words: the First of July. They was gonna release the prisoners. I went back

speaker: ES
words: to the company and on the fifth of July, we broke camp and started home.

speaker: ES
words: And I got home— landed in the United States, back American soil on

speaker: ES
words: August the fifth, 1919. That was quite a year to remember.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of impressions did you have of the French? What did they

speaker: SS
words: think of us?

speaker: ES
words: Well, (Laughter) some of the soldiers they didn't think too much of

speaker: ES
words: Well, of course, they had plenty of money and they was kinda— I

speaker: ES
words: think they thought they was a little better than anybody else. But

speaker: ES
words: they treated us good. I liked the French people.

speaker: SS
words: You say our boys did, or the French thought they were better?

speaker: ES
words: Our boys did. 'Course they thought the American army was the best paid

speaker: ES
words: army in the world to them. The French didn't get anything. They'd

speaker: ES
words: get pretty rowdy sometimes. We got along pretty good, though.

speaker: SS
words: How did the family feel about having people living in their attic?

speaker: SS
words: where I was anyway,

speaker: ES
words: Oh, they didn't object to that. They was very nice, very nicey We

speaker: ES
words: didn't have to go through the house, they just put a ladder up on the

speaker: ES
words: outside and went up to the attic. nights when I was on guard duty-

speaker: ES
words: I noticed there was always —I see a little light in the back of the

speaker: ES
words: house all the time— and they didn't allow much light there, you know.

speaker: ES
words: We was back of the lines quite a ways, but we had to be awful careful

speaker: ES
words: with the lights, you know. I'd have to walk right past this place

speaker: ES
words: where I was staying, and seems like I could see just a little dim glow

speaker: ES
words: I wondered what it was all about

speaker: ES
words: of light back there. And that night, the early morning, when I got

speaker: ES
words: off of guard duty and I started up the ladder, this old French lady

speaker: ES
words: she come out and took me by the arm and took me in the back end of the

speaker: ES
words: room there, she had some milk and bread for me when I got off of shift.

speaker: ES
words: Very nice old lady. That old French bread was pretty hard to beat.

speaker: SS
words: What about the guys that you were with in your unit?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I'll tell you. I had more trouble with them than anybody. I just

speaker: ES
words: couldn't— hard for me to adjust myself to their way of doing things.

speaker: ES
words: I take a drink but I'm not a drinker and never was. And never was a

speaker: ES
words: rowdy. I was always taught to respect. So they was kind of out of my

speaker: ES
words: kind of Me being a black,

speaker: ES
words: line. It was  to adjust myself there.. I never was around 'em any

speaker: ES
words: I left— my parents came out here when I was five years old. So I nev

speaker: ES
words: er was around any black people outside of my family until I was—

speaker: ES
words: Oh, I guess I was in high school, I went to Spokane a time or two and

speaker: ES
words: to try to get on.

speaker: ES
words: met some people up there, but— So it was quite a shock to me. And then

speaker: ES
words: I'll tell you another thing that I found out after— there was always

speaker: ES
words: a division point— After we went over, these hundred and fifty that

speaker: ES
words: went from Camp Lewis to Camp Funston, Kansas, met these guys from Camp

speaker: ES
words: Travis, some of 'em had been in the army over a year—they were all black

speaker: ES
words: but there's always kind of a division point between the Western boys

speaker: ES
words: and those that come from the South. And I found that out. My brother,

speaker: ES
words: my youngest brother, he finished college here in '29. He left high

speaker: ES
words: school as soon as he got enough credits to get in college— he didn't

speaker: ES
words: really graduate, he just as soon got enough credits and he quit, went

speaker: ES
words: to college and took a short course, dairy course, and then he went out

speaker: ES
words: and worked in creameries all over the country. Worked up at Sandpoint

speaker: ES
words: for quite a while and after he worked at that two or three years, then

speaker: ES
words: he decided to come back and finish college and he come back and finished

speaker: ES
words: college. And he finished in '29. And then he got a job at Virginia

speaker: ES
words: State College at Petersburg, Virginia. They took him on as assistant

speaker: ES
words: professor there, he just had a BS. And so he came back the next year and

speaker: ES
words: went to WSU and got his Masters, and then he got a little better job.

speaker: ES
words: Then the next summer he went to Cornell, started to work on his PhD.

speaker: ES
words: He never did finish his PhD. He finished everything but his thesis;

speaker: ES
words: He never did get his thesis. They call him Doctor Settle all the time,

speaker: ES
words: But he never did get his thesis wrote.

speaker: SS
words: That happens to a lot of people. Get everything done but that.

speaker: ES
words: He passed away before he ever got it finished at Cornell.

speaker: SS
words: He stayed there at Cornell?

speaker: ES
words: No, he just went there in the summertime, worked on his PhD. No he was

speaker: ES
words: teaching at Virginia State College at Petersburg, Virginia. But he went

speaker: ES
words: to school in Cornell in the summer months, that's he was working on

speaker: ES
words: his PhD. at Cornell.

speaker: SS
words: But when you started talking about him you were talking about the dif

speaker: SS
words: ference between Southerners and Westerners.

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah. Then he bought a big ranch down there at Petersburg,

speaker: ES
words: Six hundred and sixty-seven acres, I believe it was in this place. And

speaker: ES
words: he was quite a stockman. Course animals was what he was teaching, see.

speaker: ES
words: And he had some purebred stock. And don't you know it was hard for him

speaker: ES
words: to get help out on his farm there? Because he was a Yankee. Yeah they

speaker: ES
words: that's the truth

speaker: ES
words: didn't like him. Well, I was down there, I guess it was 1962, I was

speaker: ES
words: down there. That was my first trip down there,he had a stroke in the

speaker: ES
words: spring, so that fall I flew down to visit him. I was out there on his

speaker: ES
words: farm and a couple of old Virginia Mountaineers— he raised shorthorn

speaker: ES
words: cattle on his farm out there— and they come down there, they wanted

speaker: ES
words: to buy a heifer out of his strain, and they came down there, and I was

speaker: ES
words: there and he hadn't got out there yet, I was out there. He was having

speaker: ES
words: some work did on his house and he had me out there, I was kind of super

speaker: ES
words: vising it for him. Two old bachelors, they came down out of the hills

speaker: ES
words: up there and they wanted this heifer, they was looking for Doctor Set

speaker: ES
words: tle, and I told 'em he wasn't here yet. I said, "I'm his brother."

speaker: ES
words: "Well," he said, they didn't think I would know anything about it, but

speaker: ES
words: he had a heifer that they were interested in, they wanted some of the

speaker: ES
words: same bloodlines. And so, we talked there a while and my brother came

speaker: ES
words: and they went out to the barnyard to look at the heifer— cattle. And

speaker: ES
words: of course, my brother'd been down there— that was in '62— and he's

speaker: ES
words: been down there since '29— and one of these old guys asked him who I

speaker: ES
words: was He told  was kis brother from— "Well," he said, told him

speaker: ES
words: if he wanted to do any business with rid of him.

speaker: ES
words: You go dox-m there as soon as they open their mouth, they know you

speaker: ES
words: don't belong there. They knew I didn't belong there. It's kind of

speaker: ES
words: surprising, but it's true. And he tried to get different families

speaker: ES
words: to move out there on his place and work for him, they didn't want

speaker: ES
words: to do it.

speaker: SS
words: 'Cause they knew he was a Yankee?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, uh-huh. When I was down there, he didn't have no trouble 'cause

speaker: ES
words: he was a Southerner then. (Chuckles) And you stay there very long

speaker: ES
words: you'll acquire that drawl, too, My brother did a they had ad

speaker: ES
words: opted him by that time

speaker: ES
words: {End of Side A)

speaker: SS
words: When you were over there in Europe did you find that you had a harder

speaker: SS
words: time with the guys in your troop because you were a Westerner and not

speaker: SS
words: a Southerner?

speaker: ES
words: Well, as I say, there's kind of a little division, I had a little

speaker: ES
words: rough time,a know tha- way. I had a rough time in this way: I had

speaker: ES
words: two strikes against me. I was  the company. outfit. I was

speaker: ES
words: a Westerner and I was about one of the smallest guys in the outfit and

speaker: ES
words: I was a sergeant, too, and you know— it kind of made a difference.

speaker: ES
words: They didn't like me giving 'em orders too well, but that didn't bother

speaker: ES
words: me any. I didn't let that worry me. I give 'em just the same and I

speaker: ES
words: got 'em done, but there was some of 'em didn't like it, but there was

speaker: ES
words: n't much they could do about it. I wasn't overbearing to anybody. I

speaker: ES
words: just did my job and that was it. Whether you was from the South or

speaker: ES
words: whether you was from the West, it didn't make no difference to me. I

speaker: ES
words: treated 'em all alike, I didn't show no partiality to nobody, just did

speaker: ES
words: And for most cases I was pretty well liked.When

speaker: ES
words: my job. they told me I didn't have to go to the front, 'Course I wanted

speaker: ES
words: the guys that-were with me

speaker: ES
words: and that gave me a pull to go anyway went. r they just put up such a

speaker: ES
words: howl about me not going.

speaker: SS
words: Why were they saying you didn't have to go?

speaker: ES
words: They thought I was too young. They didn't was  sixteen.

speaker: SS
words: Did you think that the Westerners were more independent than the South

speaker: SS
words: erners? Or wasn't there any difference like that?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I don't know that there much difference. The only thing I would

speaker: ES
words: say, it seemed to me that it was just the other way around. I don't

speaker: ES
words: know whether it was the general attitude of the Southerners, but just

speaker: ES
words: because those guys from Texas, he figured if he was from Texas, there

speaker: ES
words: was nothing that compared to Texas,no way shape nor form. If you're

speaker: ES
words: from Texas, why you're a top cat!! (Chuckles) As fiscae. Idaho, they

speaker: ES
words: didn't know Idaho was on the map.

speaker: ES
words: You say anything about Washington, they'd say Washington, D.C.. It's

speaker: ES
words: a funny deal, they know about Montana, but Idaho and Washington they

speaker: ES
words: don't know nothing about it. Idaho was just a complete loss. If

speaker: ES
words: you'd say anything about Washington, they'd think you were talking

speaker: ES
words: about Washington, D.C.! But they all knew about Montana. When you'd

speaker: ES
words: say about Montana, they knew about Montana, but they didn't know about

speaker: ES
words: Washington and Idaho. Washington was Washington, D.C, and Idaho just

speaker: ES
words: didn't exist.

speaker: SS
words: That doesn't surprise me, it's still pretty bad. In the East they don't

speaker: SS
words: know.

speaker: ES
words: Yes it is. 'Course I always knew about it. When I was down there, I

speaker: ES
words: guess it was in '62 I was down there. I wanted to buy a pair of shoes,

speaker: ES
words: My brother and I

speaker: ES
words: and we was up in Richland, Virginia, a big department store there, and

speaker: ES
words: I was getting a pair of shoes, and of course,as I say a while ago, as

speaker: ES
